title: Brined Roast Turkey
subtitle: An overnight salt brine seasons the flesh and keeps the breast moist through the long roast
excerpt: Turkey breast dries faster than the legs. A wet brine overnight solves this: salt migrates through the flesh, seasoning it evenly and retaining moisture at higher temperatures. The result is a bird that stays juicy even if the oven runs hot.
sourceNotes: Nigella Lawson, Feast (2004): brined turkey method for Christmas. Delia Smith, Happy Christmas (2009): timing and resting notes. American Thanksgiving tradition adapted into British practice from the 1990s.

content.text: A whole turkey roasts for a long time. That length is non-negotiable because the legs, which need to reach 75°C, take far longer than the breast, which ideally would come off at 65°C. A
bridges this gap: it builds enough residual moisture into the breast that by the time the legs are cooked, the breast is still yielding and juicy rather than chalky and dry.

The brine itself is straightforward: salt and sugar dissolved in hot water with aromatics, cooled completely before the bird goes in. The turkey submerges in a large container or tightly sealed bag and sits in the fridge overnight. It emerges seasoned all the way through, not just on the surface.

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: at room temperature, for the breast

content.text: Make the brine and submerge the bird

in 2 litres of just-boiled water in a large saucepan. Add the bay leaves and peppercorns. Pour in 3 litres of cold water and stir. The brine must be completely cold before the bird goes in; if it is warm, refrigerate the solution first. Submerge the turkey breast-down in the brine in the largest container it will fit in, or in a sealed bag. Refrigerate for at least 12 hours, up to 24.
Remove the turkey from the brine 1 hour before cooking. Pat it thoroughly dry: this is critical for good skin. Mix the

and push it under the breast skin. Stuff the cavity with the onion and lemon halves. Lay streaky bacon across the breast if using. Set on a rack in a large roasting pan. Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan) and roast for 30 minutes to colour the skin. Reduce to 180°C (160°C fan) and continue for 20 minutes per kg. A 5 kg bird needs roughly 3.5 hours total; probe the thigh at the joint, aiming for 75°C.
Transfer to a warm board and rest for 30 minutes, covered loosely with foil and a folded tea towel to retain heat. Meanwhile, pour off the fat from the roasting pan, add
, and scrape the fond over medium heat. Simmer for 5-8 minutes, season, and strain.
during cooking is optional with a brined bird, but helps the skin colour. Carve at the table or in the kitchen; transfer breast slices and leg portions to a warm platter.

rows.fix: Pat very dry with kitchen paper, then return to the fridge uncovered for 1 hour after drying to air-dry further before roasting.
rows.cause: Bird not dried thoroughly after brining, or the brine had residual warmth when the bird went in
rows.symptom: Skin is soggy rather than crisp

rows.fix: Brine reduces risk but does not eliminate it. Use an oven thermometer. Remove the turkey when the thigh reads 75°C and accept that the breast will carry over slightly.
rows.cause: Bird was not at room temperature before roasting, or the oven ran significantly hot
rows.symptom: Breast is still overcooked despite brining

rows.fix: For a 5 kg bird, 150 g of salt in 5 litres of water for 12-18 hours is the safe range. Reduce salt to 120 g if serving an audience who prefer less seasoning.
rows.cause: Brine concentration too high, or bird was left in too long

rows.fix: Reduce the gravy for longer. The fond from a brined bird is often lighter in colour than an unbrined one; a splash of soy sauce deepens colour without detectable flavour.
rows.cause: Not enough fond in the pan, or too much liquid added too quickly

content.text: Brined turkey crossed into British cooking from American Thanksgiving practice in the 1990s and early 2000s, championed by food writers who had grown frustrated with the traditional British Christmas turkey's tendency to dryness. The technique is now mainstream. The extra step of making a brine the night before is modest; the margin it gives on the day is significant.
